speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. And the survivor is Mr. Solly Irving. Before the tape break, you were talking about when you were in Prague, when you were leaving-- when you left Prague. And you were in-- came to England with the boys, 300 of you. Whereabouts did you arrive in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lake District, Windermere We stopped off in Carlisle Airport. And we were taken by coaches to Windermere Lake District. And there, we recuperated for a couple months. It was wonderful. There was really what I call a holiday camp now. I remember distinctly the white bread. We used to call it cake with butter and ice cold milk from the farms. That was heaven. Had it not been for that, I'm sure many wouldn't-- those that have survived, had we not had the recuperation for two months, would have been-- although some of them were ill with TB and so forth, different illnesses. But that really gave us a start back to normal life. And I always said that as far as I'm concerned, my birthday is on the 14th of August, 1945, or a couple days before, from the time we came to England. Because before that, there's the early, early few couple of years what I remember of. But in between years, we weren't living. We were lucky that we survived so far.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said before, people used to ask me, how, what, and when? I don't know. I can't give any answer. I don't know myself, how did I survive. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: As my aunt said, I was running, maybe that. Maybe I was fortunate to be at the right place, to kneel in front of the German and push me to the grown ups. And we were told after, at night, people who accompanied those children, what happened to them. It's impossible to imagine anybody who has not lived. And I must tell you this. I admire people like yourself and the others who do this. It brings tears to my eyes, even now, when I think. My body shakes, not of nervousness, but of remembering what it was like. I can't describe it to you, or even how much we put in the video, what it was really like. I recently thought, how come people can deny that this ever existed when we've got a proof of myself and others-- not many others still after 50, 55 years-- still people deny. The only thing I can think of is that it's impossible for them to imagine that one human being could do this to another. Even animals to one another don't do this. And after all, we are human beings, and especially talking about a German nation, educated nation, that they could instill such hatred. I must tell you that some of the Germans weren't as bad as some of the Poles, or the Ukrainians, that went over, or the Hungarians. Everybody had to show that they can do better. And that is something that we can't pass on to others, because each one has his own story. And if people didn't go through what I went through, maybe in different ways, there were some people who suffered in Siberia. But it still wasn't the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were beaten. They were hard labored. But to do the sort of thing, to take a child by the head, and shoot it, or knock it against the wall, how can one human being do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we think of it, it's not really tears. It brings more back. I don't know. I'd like to think that my sisters didn't go through that. At least they died, death was quick, less painful. And I think of [INAUDIBLE], that I was pushed, eventually, had the sense of crying that I was a man. And that group of children, many children, who were taken out into a field, and just dug a grave, and dropped in, the German, whoever he was, Ukrainian or German, how many he could shoot, and how many he could shoot straight or whatever, or lining up two people back to back, and shoot them through the mouth. That's-- that's not human beings. I always felt that if I had the opportunity to do something like this, that I wouldn't do it quickly then. If I had the guts, I'd cut pieces of them, slowly. But we mustn't talk about that. We-- we're not here to take revenge. We're here to testify that Hitler didn't-- didn't do what he set out to do. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Very difficult, after so many years, that people still deny what happened. I'm sorry about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Windermere, then, who provided the funds for you to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it used to be called [? Bloomsbury ?] House, which is CBF, and Jewish Refugees Committee. They did all the work. They were volunteers, obviously, that came to help in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Windermere, was it-- what actually was Windermere, and who looked after you when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Windermere was a-- I don't know what it was. I think that was barracks for the army. Each one had an individual chalet, little room. And we went into that room. And we were taken there. And there was white sheets in the beds, and little cupboards standing beside it. We never experienced anything like that. Didn't even have it at home either, a single room. I think at home, I slept with my father in bed. And my sister slept with her mother. I don't believe we ever had individual beds, all shared. There, we had a little individual room. We would take on trips in boats, outings. We were running to shops, little shops. And we were buying kippers, obviously mackerel kippers. We used to eat them raw. Yeah, It was wonderful, really, really wonderful. There was a Rabbi Weiss, who eventually became Chief Rabbi of Zurich, who was the head of the religious, [? including ?] helpers. And after, we would play football. I have a picture, a visual picture, of Rabbi Weiss standing [INAUDIBLE] standing in goal, keeping goal. And there must have been 200 playing in the field. It was wonderful. It was, certainly, an eye opener. And this bread, and the Marmalade, and cold milk, that was really something. I remember the first film we saw [INAUDIBLE] They reproduced the picture in the magazine. And it came last week with that picture. It was me in front, staring, eyes [INAUDIBLE]. King Henry-- I'd never seen a film before. The home I come from, it was a very-- I wouldn't say narrow-- but orthodox home, that all we were concerned about is studying, studying, studying. And apart from playing as well, I'm sure, but [INAUDIBLE] play exactly how, what, and when, I don't remember. I started growing up when I was 15. And by then, I was already a man. So I had no youth. And that's what I regret very much-- for someone to go through life without having youth and education. I had the opportunity to study. But I was frightened. I was frightened to study. My-- my mind would go, with so many things in mind, so many bitter things to remember. I was frightened. I did have a little bit of education, not very much, in Gateshead, eventually when I left Windermere. Windermere was divided up into hostels. And each town, each city, took one or two hostel. Gateshead took a hostel. And they must have noticed that I was more religious. And they sent me to-- Gateshead was a very religious town. And we had a hostel there. Others went to other places. And there, I had a little bit of education, not much. But they couldn't make up for the loss. And my religious knowledge is quite good. Only, I think, I'm sure mostly what I remember from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you sent to Gateshead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months after being in Windermere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you sent with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a group of boys. There was [NON-ENGLISH]. There was [NON-ENGLISH], so many others. 20 of us went, the Windermere brothers, [NON-ENGLISH]. Unfortunately, some have died. [NON-ENGLISH] died. [NON-ENGLISH] died. And then from there, people went to families, and went different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you taught when you were at Gateshead?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- teachers used to come in to try and teach us English, and math.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you left Gateshead, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I left Gateshead-- actually, I came a couple of times. I came to London, and stayed in Highbury New Park Hostel for a while, and went back to Gateshead, and then came back to London. And, eventually, I-- as I said before, Mr. Friedman, of the committee, wanted me to go to college. He wasn't a religious man. And I was registered to go [? Haslemere ?] College to study. He bought me tickets to train. In those days, we used to get 10 shillings, a half a pound, pocket money a week. And you used to buy a lot of things for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much is that equivalent to nowadays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was 50 pence. But it used to buy a lot more than I think, I'm sure, 10 pounds could buy today. And he registered me to go to college. I got as far as the train, to the station, and came back. I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go to study. I wanted to start doing work. And I didn't want to rely on people. All our ambition was to be able to stand on our own feet. It was very difficult for us to settle down to-- to-- to do things, to be told what to do. We were men already. Windermere, when they were passing through beginning, when they were passing school the meals, we were sitting at long benches, long tables. And they couldn't understand, as we were passing through the meals from beginning table to the end, kept on going around. As we were given the meal, we put it on the table. We didn't realize that we were going to get meals in the evening, and in the next morning, and we don't have to hide. We must have been very difficult people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the people that looked after you? Were they nurses or were they teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were nurses. Some where, I'm sure, leaders of groups of certain-- I remember distinctly, [NON-ENGLISH] was a religious man. Israel Cohen, who was a teacher [INAUDIBLE] for years, who lives now in Israel. His present wife that wasn't married then was a nurse. And I had laryngitis. We started singing, and eventually voice broke. And I had terrible trouble. It broke on Yom Kippur, I remember. We had service. And we were singing. And she nursed me. She gave me steam towels and steam, to bring me back to-- yeah, they were wonderful people, wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then from the Highbury New Park Hostel, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I registered-- well I went-- I registered myself, eventually got to Israel. And I think from Highbury New Park, I went back to Gateshead, and came back to London. I can't remember where I stayed in London. I can't remember. But, anyway, I registered myself, and got to Israel, [INAUDIBLE] Palestine [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I had to wait. And the funny thing was, every time we went to register in [INAUDIBLE] or in Swiss Cottage there was a place, we always asked for the same person. And it was never the same person, for security. And, unfortunately, it was illegal. I had to wait till I was 18, till August '48-- or just before, it was actually July, I think-- to go to Israel, register myself. I already had contact with my aunt in Israel. And I already had contact with my uncle also through letters. I met somebody in lodge who was my aunt's brother, who died recently in Brazil. He-- he was liberated before me. And he was older than me. And he remembered their names and everything. And he wrote to them. So he gave me the address. When I came to England, I asked someone to write for me a letter. I wrote Yiddish. I could write Yiddish. But I think I asked somebody. My aunt contacted me through Mr. Goodman, who was then the head of [INAUDIBLE] school. He went to a conference in Germany just after the war. And my aunt approached him, and told him that she was looking for her nephew. And she thinks he went to England. And he brought me a letter. So I made contact with her. And she, eventually, went to Israel also. And I met up with her when I went in '48. I was, obviously, enlisted straightaway into the army. We went through Marseilles. In the story, we weren't officially. Obviously, I had to apply for a travel document. We weren't British. I got a green travel document lasting for a year. I applied for a holiday visa to go to Switzerland. And even [INAUDIBLE] transit visa to stop off in France. [NON-ENGLISH] Switzerland. In France, we were given certain addresses to contact in Paris, who bought us tickets to go to Marseilles. And from Marseilles, we waited for the illegal boat. And that's how we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the boat's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], I've got a picture with, actually-- was taken by [INAUDIBLE], who I met up with. We were beginning of the war, together. And then some of us separated. And [NON-ENGLISH], he came from Germany to go to Israel, Palestine. And I came from England. And we met up and we stayed together. And I'm still very close in touch. Unfortunately, he's got, developed, Parkinson's, which is not a very pleasant illness. But surprising, whenever I go to meet him, he lives in [INAUDIBLE], his wife thinks that he's as normal as normal can be when we are together. And we talk about old times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get to Israel then?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with when you were on the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we met up-- I met up with another friend of mine, this friend [NON-ENGLISH], and another friend, [NON-ENGLISH], it was also one of our boys. He also decided to go to [INAUDIBLE] and several others we met up. And there was quite a lot of people. Then they took on illegal immigrants as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back a bit, the 300 boys, and then there were more so it made 732, were they all boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were some girls, because of the boys, even the girls. There weren't many girls. It wasn't easy for a girl to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, sorry, then to move forward again to Israel. When you got to Haifa--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken off the boat before Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were taken to camp, at a place called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's an army camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an army camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Marseilles, we were given sort of a few minutes training, you could call it, with sticks, not real arms. And then, as I got into the camp, we enlisted. And we were given a couple of days leave. So I went to look for my uncle. I went to Haifa. And they live [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found them, yes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, afterwards, when you left the army, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't leave the army. I didn't leave the army. I was enlisted into the army, together with my friend [NON-ENGLISH]. We were-- we were not taken into the Anglo-Saxon-- there was Anglo-Saxons, quite a few of our boys in the 300, and the others were volunteers. And we weren't taken, because we were-- I was mixed up with him, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we spoke Yiddish. We didn't go particularly with the Anglo-Saxons. So we were enlisted into the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the [INAUDIBLE] brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, as I said, we had a couple days off. And then we were taken-- then we were taken on tours to get ourselves acclimatized. And then we had a little bit of training in the camp. And we were taken-- my camp was taken into a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is on the Gaza area, kibbutz. And it was also a-- what we call a [NON-ENGLISH], an observation post. And we were in the army, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost-- but not in that particular place. In that place, we were in till we captured [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And wen was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was September, September, October in '48. I was one of the first who went to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any funny experiences whilst being in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [LAUGHS], in that particular camp, in that particular place, called [NON-ENGLISH], first of all, they used to grill a lot of onions. [INAUDIBLE]. And, yet, we had a group of Moroccans who joined our group. And at night, each one had a gun. But there was only 10 bullets amongst the group. So you only had one bullet, particularly, or two bullets, to put in there. And the Sergeant told us to put-- to cock one bullet in, but not to put it in the pipe, just to leave it in, in case you needed it. And one of the Moroccans fired, and all dispersed, like Arabs, all dispersed, run. We had, for hours, trying to find him. That's, yeah, amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the experience later on in a certain place overlooking strategic points. And down below-- this was already in '49, [INAUDIBLE] '49, just before [INAUDIBLE] '49, I remember that. We were in a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a notorious place-- not the actual place, but the strategic point we were keeping an eye out for, and guarding it. And below us was a field of watermelons. And I decided to walk down and get a watermelon. And I was fired at in all directions by Egyptians. I had to lie low until the help came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you hurt at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take for help to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been a half hour, an hour, till they came. I wasn't adventurous anymore after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]. Were there any other experiences in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: We captured, once, an Arab officer, with his car. And we had some prisoners [INAUDIBLE]. And one of the prisoners, obviously, had never seen a radio, heard a radio. And when we put the radio in the car on to listen to the news, he didn't know where it was coming from. So he laid underneath the car to see where the voice was coming from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you left [NON-ENGLISH], what did you do from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was sent to another camp called [NON-ENGLISH], for recuperating. I developed an ulcer. And I was sent for recuperating with an ulcer break, because we were away for some time. And there I met-- it was near [NON-ENGLISH]. And there I met an old friend, my old friend, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we got pictures taken with him in [NON-ENGLISH] Park, which doesn't exist now. And what was [NON-ENGLISH] doing out there? [NON-ENGLISH] also-- I don't know how he came into-- because he was with the Anglo-Saxons as a volunteer. I don't know how he came to our camp. He must have been-- but before that, it's a very important little story I forgot. We were in one camp. And in those days, as you arrived from the boats, [? Latrun ?], a very notorious place-- most strategic points, we had police stations were built along citadels in the strategic areas. [? Latrun ?] was up on the hill. [INAUDIBLE] almost take, in those days, people from the boat without any experience, and sent into [? Latrun ?]. And many, many got killed. There were-- we were in one camp, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had-- there was an order to assemble. And we were going to be shipped to [NON-ENGLISH]. Whether it was a [INAUDIBLE], or what, I can't remember. And [NON-ENGLISH] and I looked at each other. And we said-- it was already known then it was an infamous place, notorious. And we looked at each other. And we said, it's not for us. So we're trying to find how to get out of it. And we decided we were going to hide. It's not very nice of us, after we came to fight in the army. I don't know whether it flashed through that I just try and stay alive or what, I didn't know. But anyway, we decided we were not going to turn up. And we didn't. The camp was taken out on-- on-- on wagons, transporters, transporters, army transporters, to [NON-ENGLISH]. And we managed to not turn up. After a while, we were walking through the camp. There were, obviously, some people still behind. And we were arrested, put into prison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] My luck, who should be the Sergeant in charge of the guard house, is a boy called [NON-ENGLISH]. If you remember, [NON-ENGLISH] was my home town who spent the times in the ghetto in our house. He saw us. He knew us.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you doing here? We told him the story. And he said, you better try and find somewhere else to go. [INAUDIBLE] said go, go to another camp. Turn up somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, unfortunately, that-- the majority of the people who went there to [? Latrun ?] didn't come back. So there is, again, a little story that-- whether I was destined to stay alive, I don't know. It's a bit unpleasant to say that we didn't want to do-- we made out that we don't eat kosher. And we don't [INAUDIBLE] in the army. It wasn't-- but that's what we did. And we're here. It's a little-- it might not be in sequence. But these little stories that you hear from me today that some are pleasant, and some unpleasant. I suppose it's unpleasant to tell that I disobeyed orders. But, nevertheless, I must have a vision that I wanted to stay alive. That's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we've just come to the end of tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was--
